---
title: ""
page-layout: full
---

::: {.hero-panel}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
#### Ordered paths for spectral-line morphology {.hero-kicker}

# spectropath {.hero-title}

A small R package for turning spectral lines into named geometric diagnostics.
Treat a line profile as an ordered path \(X = (u, F)\), then compute
low-order path-signature coordinates that respond to asymmetry, wings,
shoulders, double peaks, and emission--absorption ordering.

[Quick start](#quick-start){.hero-button .hero-button-primary}
[Standalone examples](examples.html){.hero-button}
[Function reference](reference.html){.hero-button}
[GitHub](https://github.com/RafaelSdeSouza/spectropath){.hero-button}
:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
::: {.output-card}
Minimal output

```r
library(spectropath)

u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

path_features(path)
#      p2  p_pm    p3u    p3F    p4F    p4T
# -0.3133 0.000 -0.0169 -0.1736 0.4534 0.0132
```
:::
:::
::::
:::

## What spectropath returns

The package exposes the paper-facing coordinates and a compact set of
classical line-profile summaries for benchmarking.

| Output | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| `p2` | signed velocity--flux area |
| `p3u` | where asymmetry lies along the ordered coordinate |
| `p3F` | whether asymmetry is concentrated in bright or faint parts of the line |
| `p4F` | higher-order flux modulation |
| `p4T` | twist-like higher-order structure |
| `p_pm` | emission--absorption ordering |

## Quick start {#quick-start}

Install from GitHub:

```r
install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("RafaelSdeSouza/spectropath")
library(spectropath)
```

From a local checkout:

```r
remotes::install_local("/Users/rd23aag/Documents/GitHub/spectropath")
```

Compute both path and classical summaries from the same line profile:

```r
u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

path_features(path)
classical_features(path)
```

| `path_features(path)` | Value |
|---|---:|
| `p2` | `-0.3133` |
| `p_pm` | `0.0000` |
| `p3u` | `-0.0169` |
| `p3F` | `-0.1736` |
| `p4F` | `0.4534` |
| `p4T` | `0.0132` |

| `classical_features(path)` | Value |
|---|---:|
| `line_flux` | `1.2500` |
| `equivalent_width` | `1.2500` |
| `peak_flux` | `0.3990` |
| `fwhm` | `3.3267` |
| `w80` | `3.0746` |
| `centroid` | `0.3600` |
| `line_dispersion` | `1.1657` |
| `skewness` | `-0.0815` |
| `kurtosis_excess` | `-0.6044` |

## Two visual anchors

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="66%"}
![Cropped toy-line comparison strip from the current repository figures.](site-assets/toy_case_row1.png)
:::
::: {.column width="34%"}
![Single-galaxy kinematic crop from the current repository figures.](site-assets/kinematics_col1_maps.png)
:::
::::

These are the repository-local assets the site actually uses. The first shows
the profile-comparison logic used in the paper discussion of line-shape
discrimination. The second is a cropped IFU-facing kinematic example, kept
narrower so the homepage stays clean instead of showing the full multi-panel
block.

More examples live on the [Examples](examples.html) page.
